coredata.dc:title: Rapid detoxification of polluted water using ultrastable TiO2 encapsulated CsPbBr3 QDs in collected sunlight
coredata.dc:creator: Deshpande, S.V.
Bhiungade, R.A.
Deshpande, M.P.
Pawar, K.K.
Bhat, T.S.
Kulkarni, S.K.
Sheikh, Arif D.
coredata.dc:description: The CsPbBr3 perovskite quantum dots (CPB PQDs) was prepared using the anti-solvent precipitation method in ambient air. The low water stability of CPB PQDs hinders photocatalytic applications, so colloidal CPB PQDs were encapsulated and protected with TiO2. The thickness of TiO2 encapsulation was controlled by immersing the same volume of CPB PQDs into different concentrations of Titanium (Ti) precursor. The optimum CPB/TiO2 composition degrades 78% Methylene Blue (MB) after 300 min under natural (diffuse) sunlight. Furthermore, the same photocatalyst detoxifies MB more than 99% after 50 min illumination of collected sunlight. This outstanding photocatalysis in collected sunlight is attributed to the improved rate of photoinduced charge carriers and their efficient separation due to favourable band alignment of CPB PQD with TiO2. This work provides a pathway to implement an efficient photocatalyst strategy for environmental remediation in outdoor conditions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CsPbBr3
Perovskite QD
Nanocomposite
Photocatalysis
Dye degradation
Sunlight

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Structural and optical properties of CsPbBr3 PQDs
Structural and morphological study of CsPbBr3/TiO2 nanocomposite particles
UV-visible absorbance spectra of dye degradation under natural sunlight
A possible mechanism and stability test for photocatalytic reaction

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Science and Technology of India

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The author ADS would like to thank the Department of Science and Technology (DST), Ministry of Science and Technology of India , for the research grant and DST-INSPIRE Faculty Award No. DST/INSPIRE/04/2015/002601 . The author SKK thanks INSA, N. Delhi, India, for INSA Sr. Scientist position. The authors are thankful to Dr. Dattatray Late, NCL Pune for providing TEM facility.

The author ADS would like to thank the Department of Science and Technology (DST), Ministry of Science and Technology of India, for the research grant and DST-INSPIRE Faculty Award No. DST/INSPIRE/04/2015/002601. The author SKK thanks INSA, N. Delhi, India, for INSA Sr. Scientist position. The authors are thankful to Dr. Dattatray Late, NCL Pune for providing TEM facility.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Important steps involved in the synthesis of CsPbBr3/TiO2 nanocomposite powder using anti-solvent precipitation method.

(a) X-ray diffraction pattern, (b) UV–Vis absorbance and photoluminescence (PL) spectra (c) Tauc plot of CsPbBr3 PQDs. (d) Photograph of CsPbBr3 PQDs colloidal solution before (left) and after (right) UV illumination.
X-Ray diffraction pattern of CPB PQD, CPB@10-TiO2, CPB@30-TiO2 and CPB@50-TiO2 nanocomposite particles.

The FESEM images of (a) CPB PQD, (b) CPB@10-TiO2, (c) CPB@30-TiO2 and (d) CPB@50-TiO2 nanoparticles drop-casted on FTO substrates. (e) The TEM image of CPB@30-TiO2 nanoparticles and corresponding SAED pattern is shown in inlet.

UV–Vis absorbance spectra indicating the photocatalytic degradation of MB using (a) pristine TiO2, (b) CPB@10-TiO2, (c) CPB@30-TiO2, and (d) CPB@50-TiO2 nanocomposite photocatalyst in natural sunlight.
Comparative variation of (a)% degradation of MB and (b) kinetic rate constant for pristine TiO2, CPB@10-TiO2, CPB@30-TiO2, and CPB@50-TiO2 nanocomposite in natural sunlight.

(a) Schematic representation of photodegradation experimental arrangement in collected sunlight. (b) UV–Vis absorbance spectra indicating diluted MB concentration using CPB@30-TiO2 under collected sunlight. Time dependent comparative variation of (c)% degradation of MB and (d) kinetic rate constant of CPB@30-TiO2 in natural (diffuse) and collected (intense) sunlight.

(a) Transient photocurrent response of drop-casted CPB@30-TiO2 thin film on FTO substrate in ONOFF cycle of different illumination intensities. Schematic illustration of the mechanism of charge generation and separation in (b) natural and (c) collected sunlight. (d) XRD of CPB@30-TiO2 nanocomposite before (black) and after photocatalysis process under natural (red) and collected (blue) sunlight. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The literature survey of degradation rate of different chemicals under the action of perovskite halide materials. [GB = Glove Box, D = degradation; TL = Light illumination time; MO = methylene orange; RhB = Rhodamine B; BA = Benzyl alcohol; 7ACA = 7-aminocephalosporanic acid.].

head.ce:title: Rapid detoxification of polluted water using ultrastable TiO2 encapsulated CsPbBr3 QDs in collected sunlight

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Nanoscience and Technology, Shivaji University, Kolhapur, 416004, M.S., India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Nanoscience and Technology, Shivaji University, Kolhapur, 416004, M.S., India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Materials for Electronics Technology (CMET), Panchwati, Pune 411008, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre for Materials for Electronics Technology (CMET)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bCentre for Materials for Electronics Technology (CMET), Panchwati, Pune 411008, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The CsPbBr3 perovskite quantum dots (CPB PQDs) was prepared using the anti-solvent precipitation method in ambient air. The low water stability of CPB PQDs hinders photocatalytic applications, so colloidal CPB PQDs were encapsulated and protected with TiO2. The thickness of TiO2 encapsulation was controlled by immersing the same volume of CPB PQDs into different concentrations of Titanium (Ti) precursor. The optimum CPB/TiO2 composition degrades 78% Methylene Blue (MB) after 300 min under natural (diffuse) sunlight. Furthermore, the same photocatalyst detoxifies MB more than 99% after 50 min illumination of collected sunlight. This outstanding photocatalysis in collected sunlight is attributed to the improved rate of photoinduced charge carriers and their efficient separation due to favourable band alignment of CPB PQD with TiO2. This work provides a pathway to implement an efficient photocatalyst strategy for environmental remediation in outdoor conditions.

ce:section.ce:para: Water pollution is a formidable problem due to the generation of wastewater containing hazardous organic dyes from the growing industrialization and often expose to environmental water sources. Particularly, the textiles industry utilizes different types of highly toxic dyes having non-biodegradable aromatic structure. This influences human health and aquatic biota, so it is essential to detoxify these organic pollutants for protecting people and the environment [1,2]. Conventional wastewater treatments like adsorption, ultrasonic decomposition, electrocoagulation, chemical coagulation, nano-filtration and sedimentation are insufficient. These techniques have some deficiencies, including high energy consumption, expensive, and production of secondary pollutants [3]. Therefore, sunlight driven detoxification of polluted water with nanostructured semiconducting photocatalyst provides an effective solution to environmental remediation [4–9]. Till now, plenty of efforts devoted to identify low-cost, stable and efficient photocatalyst, such as TiO2 [10–19], In2O3 [20], ZnS [21] and CdS [22,23]. Among these wide bandgap semiconductors, TiO2 remained the most popular photocatalytic material due to its exceptional chemical and thermal stability. But the wide bandgap of the TiO2 limited to absorption in the UV region (~ 5%), while the visible photon energy is not fully utilized [24]. As a result, various ways attempted to enhance the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 by making its heterojunctions with metal and carbon-based materials [24,25]. Apart from this, some popular low bandgap metal oxides like Fe2O3 [26] BiFeO3 [27], BiVO4 [28–30], and ZnFe2O4 [31–33] show promising photocatalytic activity in visible light. However, the development of efficient, robust and low-cost photocatalyst has never ceased in the last few decades.

Until now, various hybrid perovskite materials have attracted widespread attention in various [34–43] applications. Apart from this, numerous efforts have taken to design and prepare hybrid perovskite halide materials for photocatalysis applications because of their superior optical, chemical and optoelectronic properties [44–46]. Gao et al. fabricated CsPbX3 (where X = Cl, Br, and I) QDs by an emulsion technique for the photocatalytic degradation of methyl orange (MO) solution [47]. They found that CsPbCl3 (CPC) and CsPbBr3 (CPB) QDs could degrade 90% and 82% of MO solution, respectively, under 80 min of visible light irradiation. Paul et al. reported the synthesis of g-C3N4 CsPbBrCl2 based type-II semiconductor heterojunction for degradation complex organic effluents under visible-light illumination [44]. Recently water-stable nanocomposite of CPB perovskite / TiO2 heterojunction successfully demonstrated photocatalytic applications [7,48]. This suggested that the metal halide perovskite-based photocatalysis field is still in a relatively early discovery stage. Hence, it is necessary to explore more metal halide perovskite for the effective detoxification of water.

The most popular metal halide perovskites contain the heavy element lead (Pb), which is toxic. Hence during the water purification process, it is important to avoid possible Pb contamination. Therefore, encapsulation of wide bandgap metal oxides semiconductors could solve this problem by protecting the surface decomposition. Moreover, TiO2 encapsulated CPB PQDs offers several advantages in the context of photocatalysis like i) maintaining the original structural properties, ii) preventing the aggregation, iii) protect the CPB PQDs surface from destabilizing polar molecules, iv) more importantly shields the environment from Pb toxicity and finally v) efficiently transfers the charge carriers to minimize the recombination process. Therefore, it is necessary to understand any impact of the TiO2 encapsulation thickness on the charge transport and the photocatalytic process. As a result, this work investigates photocatalytic detoxification of polluted water using CPB/TiO2 nanocomposite particles with different TiO2 composition. Additionally, the photocatalytic activity of CPB/TiO2 nanocomposite was studied outdoor in natural (diffuse) and collected (intense) sunlight. Our results demonstrate that CPB/TiO2 nanocomposite degrades 78% of MB in 300 min of natural sunlight exposure. But under the collected sunlight illumination, the same photocatalyst detoxifies over 99% of MB in just 50 min. The excellent photocatalytic activity of CPB/TiO2 nanocomposite under collected sunlight is attributed to increased absorption, large photogenerated charge carriers, and efficient migration of charge carriers at the photocatalyst surface due to the suitable energy band position between CPB and TiO2. The current state of the art demonstrates that this emerging all-inorganic perovskite halide photocatalyst holds promise towards a low-cost and effective solution to address environmental issues.

Roo et al. investigated more insights regarding complex reactions and surface chemistry during the synthesis of CPB PQDs [49]. Fig. 1 shows schematic illustrations of steps involved in the synthesis procedure of CPB/TiO2 nanocomposite. The synthesis of CPB/TiO2 nanocomposite was performed into two major parts. In the first part, CPB PQDs were synthesized by the anti-solvent assisted precipitation method with modified synthesis temperature [50]. Further, the CPB/TiO2 nanocomposite was prepared, and the thickness of the TiO2 tuned with different TBOT precursor concentrations. The details were explained as follows.

All the chemical reagents used in this synthesis were procured from Sigma Aldrich without any purification. The synthesis of CPB involved preparing cesium olate (Cs-oleate), PbBr2 solutions and using them to make CPB PQDs as follows.

0.04 g of Cs2CO3, 1.5 ml of Octadecene (ODE) (C18H36) and 0.12 ml of Oleic acid (OA) (C18H34O2) were mixed into a beaker using a stirrer. The temperature of this solution was maintained at 120 °C. The disappearance of all Cs2CO3 solids indicated the formation of Cs-oleate (Cs-OA). The Cs-oleate solution was sealed in a vial with paraffin foil and stored at room temperature for further use, as shown in Fig. 1. The chemical reaction during the formation of Cs-oleate is shown in Eq. (1) as follows,
(1) CsCO3+OA +ODE→ Cs-OA+CO2

PbBr2 solution: 0.03 g of lead bromide (PbBr2), 2 ml of dimethylformamide (DMF), 0.023 g of hexanoic acid (C6H12O2) (HA) and 0.025 g of Octylamine (C8H19N) (OAm) were mixed and stirred at 70 °C temperature until getting the clear solution also shown in Fig. 1. The formation of Pb-oleate (Pb-OA) and oleylammonium bromide (OAm-Br) is shown in the following Eq. (2),
(2) PbBr2+HA + OAm → Pb-OA +OAm-Br

CsPbBr3 PQDs: 0.1 ml of Cs-oleate solution was added to PbBr2 solution in a flask. After that, 0.5 ml of the Cs-oleate and PbBr2 solution mixture added into 10 ml of anhydrous toluene. The reaction mixture slowly turned into the yellow colour solution indicating precipitation of CPB PQDs. The growth of the CPB PQDs was stopped by quenching the flask of yellow coloured colloidal solution into the ice bath. The solution was centrifuged for 5 min at 5000 rpm to remove aggregates and substantial larger particles. After this step, the obtained PQDs in the form of residue was washed with ethyl acetate. Finally, the obtained CPB PQDs were stored by re-dispersing them in toluene and sealed until further use. The possible chemical reaction with which CPB PQDs formed is shown in Eq. (3).
(3) Cs-OA + Pb-OA + 3OAm-Br → CsPbBr3+ (OA)2 3OAm

Here, Titanium butoxide (TBOT) was used as the titanium precursor. First, different amounts of TBOT precursor such as 10 µl, 30 µl and 50 µl were separately added to 1 ml of toluene. This 1 ml TBOT-toluene solution was slowly added in 10 ml of colloidal CPB PQDs solution, as shown in Fig. 1. After stirring, this solution was kept stable for 3 h at room temperature. During this time, the hydrolysis reaction started to coat TiOx on the CPB PQDs. After 3 h, the precipitate was collected through centrifugation at 5000 rpm for 20 min, followed by drying at 80 °C. The dry powder was calcined at 300 °C for 5 h to obtain CPB/TiO2 nanocomposite particles, as shown in Fig. 1. For simplicity, the prepared samples were labelled as CPB@10-TiO2, CPB@30-TiO2 and CPB@50-TiO2 nanocomposite. For example, the sample labelled with CPB@10-TiO2 nanocomposite was synthesized by adding 1 ml of toluene containing 10 µl of TBOT into 10 ml of colloidal CPB PQDs.

The samples synthesized as in the previous section were subjected to various characterization techniques as follows. The X-ray diffractometer (XRD Model: Bruker X-ray powder diffractometer with CuKα radiation = 1.5418 Å) was used to understand the crystalline nature and phase purity of the CPB QDs and CPB@10-TiO2, CPB@30-TiO2 and CPB@50-TiO2 nanocomposite particles powder. The microstructure of the CPB QDs and CPB@10-TiO2, CPB@30-TiO2 and CPB@50-TiO2 nanocomposites was identified using Field Emission-Scanning Electron Microscope (FE-SEM Model No. S4200NHITACHI). High-resolution Transmission Electron Microscopy (HR-TEM Model FEI Tecnai 300) equipped with Energy Dispersive X-Ray (EDX) was used to investigate the morphology and evaluate elemental compositions. For TEM analysis, the dilute colloidal solution of the samples was drop-casted on C-coated Cu grids (300 mesh). Photoluminescence (PL) study of CPB PQDs and nanocomposite colloidal solution performed using Bruker Spectrofluorometer (Model Fluromax 4). The concentration of MB solution was identified using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Model: BioEra Life Sciences Pvt. Ltd. India) in the wavelength range 450 nm - 800 nm.

In the first step, the photocatalytic activity of the CPB@10-TiO2, CPB@30-TiO2 and CPB@50-TiO2 nanocomposite study was carried out under the irradiation of natural sunlight. In this experiment, 20 ml of MB dye solution (concentration of 10 ppm / L) with 10 mg of the photocatalyst was used for photocatalysis. This solution was sonicated for 10 min to reduce agglomeration and enhance the uniform dispersion of nanoparticles. The quantity of adsorbed organic pollutant on the surface of photocatalyst NPs is a crucial factor that decides their photocatalytic performance. Therefore, this solution was stirred for 30 min before sunlight illumination for achieving good adsorption/desorption equilibrium between dye and photocatalyst. The adsorption process was confirmed by measuring the UV–Vis spectra of MB solution, as shown in Fig. S1 of supporting information. After this step, even if we keep this solution in the dark for a longer time, there is no change in the absorption of MB dye. This indicates that during this process, the dye molecules were adsorbed on the surface of the nanoparticles. This collected MB solution was treated as 0 min of sunlight illumination. The photocatalytic activity of all the CPB@TiO2 nanoparticles was carried out under sunlight illumination at outdoor conditions. The sunlight assisted degradation process was monitored by taking out the solution after each fixed interval of time. When the photocatalytic experiment was performed under natural and intense sunlight, the MB solution was collected after each 60 min and 10 min of illumination, respectively. This was continued until the MB solution colour changes from blue to colourless. Finally, all the collected MB solutions were centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 5 min to settle down the colloidal nanoparticles. The concentration of the collected MB solution was analysed using a UV-Vis spectrophotometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and optical properties of CsPbBr3 PQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The XRD pattern of CPB PQDs is shown in Fig. 2 a. The XRD peaks are observed at 2θ = 21.6°, 30.7°, 34.4°, 37.7° and 43.8° which correspond to (110), (200), (201), (211) and (202) diffraction planes respectively. The XRD pattern demonstrates that no impurity is available in the synthesized CPB phase. All the XRD peaks are consistent with monoclinic CPB, which agrees with the JCPDS No. 18–0364. The monoclinic phase of the CPB PQDs is well discriminated by splitting a diffraction peak at 2θ ~31° The lattice parameter extracted for CPB PQD is 5.83 Å. These results demonstrate that CPB PQDs synthesized in an ambient air environment possess high crystalline quality. Thus a highly crystalline monoclinic, single-phase perovskite structure of CPB PQDs has been achieved.

The optical properties of the colloidal CPB PQDs solution were investigated by measuring UV–Vis absorption and PL data. The UV–Vis absorbance and photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CPB PQDs colloidal solution is shown in Fig 2b. The UV–Vis spectra show that the CPB PQDs absorb in the wavelength range of 350 to 700 nm. Fig. 2b illustrates the characteristic PL peak of the CPB PQDs is observed at ~ 520 nm. The PL peak of CPB PQDs is symmetric and slightly broad Gaussian in shape. The estimated optical bandgap for the Tauc plot (Fig. 2c) of CPB PQDs is 2.3 eV, in good agreement with the literature [48]. Fig. 2d shows the photographic representation of CPB PQDs colloidal solution in the ambient visible light (left side) and UV light (right side).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural and morphological study of CsPbBr3/TiO2 nanocomposite particles
ce:section.ce:para: The effects of encapsulation of TiO2 on the crystal structure of CPB@TiO2 has been studied by the XRD pattern. The comparative XRD patterns of CPB QD, CPB@10-TiO2, CPB@30-TiO2, and CPB@50-TiO2 nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 3 . As observed, the XRD pattern of CPB@10-TiO2 exhibits similar diffraction peaks belonging to the CPB phase, but peaks belonging to the TiO2 phase do not appear due to its negligible concentration. Nevertheless, a clear diffraction peak is observed at 2θ ~25.2° in the XRD patterns of CPB@30-TiO2 and CPB@50-TiO2 nanocomposite assigning to the anatase phase of TiO2 in addition to CPB peaks. The XRD patterns of CPB@30-TiO2 and CPB@50-TiO2 nanocomposite contains the diffraction peaks that belong to both CPB and TiO2. The individual peaks of the CPB and TiO2 phase confirm that the CPB does not react with TBOT during the synthesis and annealing process. We also observed that no structural phase transition occurred in the perovskite structure of CPB QDs upon the encapsulation process. As discussed earlier, the (002) and (200) dual diffraction planes belong to inter-reticular planes of the monoclinic system. This dual diffraction peak becomes slightly shifted closer to each other and sharper in the XRD patterns of all the CPB@TiO2 nanocomposites. The peak sharpness represents improved crystallization growth of CPB PQDs during the annealing stage. The shift in the (002) peak position can be either related to thermal cell expansion of CPB or distortion in a preferred orientation retard the crystal growth of the CPB phase during the annealing process [51]. The average crystallite size of pristine CPB PQDs, CPB@10-TiO2, CPB@30-TiO2 and CPB@50-TiO2 is 10 nm, 14 nm, 16 nm and 17 nm, respectively. The increase in the average crystallite size with an increase in the concentration of TBOT suggests increased thickness of TiO2 coating around CPB PQDs.

The particle size of the photocatalyst is a crucial factor, as the oxidation and reduction reactions take place on the photocatalyst surface. Therefore particle size plays an important role in determining the efficiency of photocatalytic activity. The morphology of CPB@TiO2 nanocomposite particles drop cast on the Fullerene doped Tin Oxide (FTO) substrates was studied using the FESEM technique and shown in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4a and Fig. 4b show the morphology of CPB PQD and CPB@10-TiO2 thin films, whereas Fig. 4c and 4d show the FESEM images CPB@30-TiO2 and CPB@50-TiO2 thin films, respectively. The FESEM images indicate that the dried powder of CPB PQDs and CPB@TiO2 nanoparticles are agglomerated on the substrate. The encapsulation process of CPB PQDs with TiO2 could remove the surface ligands, which cause particle agglomeration. The average particle size extracted from particle size distribution curves measured for all CPB@TiO2 nanocomposites. The particle size distribution curves, average particle size and corresponding surface area to volume ratio are mentioned in supporting information Table S1. The average particle size for CPB@10TiO2, CPB@30TiO2 and CPB@50TiO2 nanocomposites is 13 nm, 20 nm and 25 nm, respectively. Further, the surface area to volume ratio calculated for CPB@10TiO2, CPB@30TiO2 and CPB@50TiO2 nanocomposites is 0.48 /nm, 0.30 /nm and 0.23 /nm, respectively. This indicates that the amount of TiO2 precursor in colloidal CPB QDs solution defines the particle size of the nanocomposite.

Further, the morphology and composition of the CPB@30-TiO2 nanocomposites were investigated using the TEM technique. The TEM image of the CPB@30-TiO2 nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 4e. The TEM image shows that the CPB@30-TiO2 exhibits spherical morphology with an average diameter of ~ 10 nm. Further, the marked region indicates a formation of core-shell type structure as individual particles of CPB surrounded by TiO2 nanoparticles. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern of the CPB@30-TiO2 nanoparticles is shown in the inlet of Fig. 4e. The EDX spectra of CPB@10-TiO2, CPB@30-TiO2 and CPB@50-TiO2 nanocomposite sample are shown in Fig. S2 (a-c) of supporting information. Their corresponding weight percentages of the constituting elements are represented in the tabular form shown in Fig. S2 d. The EDX spectra of CPB@10-TiO2, CPB@30-TiO2 and CPB@50-TiO2 nanocomposite show Ti and O elements along with Cs, Pb and Br with different weight concentrations. As expected, the weight% of Ti and O was lower for CPB@10-TiO2 and higher for CPB@50-TiO2 nanocomposites. We noticed a significant reduction in the Cs, Pb and Br content in the CPB@50-TiO2 nanocomposite sample, indicating that the high shell thickness of the TiO2 lowers the penetration depth of electrons.

ce:section.ce:section-title: UV-visible absorbance spectra of dye degradation under natural sunlight
ce:section.ce:para: In contrast to previous reports [47], our results indicated that CPB PQDs are inherently unstable in water like most other organic-inorganic halide perovskites, in agreement with the literature [48]. Before performing the photocatalysis experiment, the water tolerance test was carried out by immersing CPB PQDs and CPB@TiO2 nanocomposite powders in water. To visualize the changes, the photographic images of the CPB PQDs in toluene and water is shown in Fig. S3a and Fig. S3b, respectively. The as-synthesized CPB PQDs dispersed in toluene solution remain stable in the colloidal form. But these yellow coloured PQDs are converted into a white colloidal solution once immersed in water (Fig. S3b). This indicates that CPB is not stable in water and lost its optical properties. Fig. S3c shows the photographic representation of CPB@TiO2 nanocomposite in water remains stable. The structural instability of the CPB PQDs in water was confirmed using the XRD technique. Fig. S4 of supporting information shows the comparative XRD of dried CPB PQDs powder before and after immersed in water. The XRD pattern of CPB PQDs in water shows significant variation in peak position and intensities compared to that of the prepared CPB PQDs powder. Particularly the intensities of (100), (110) and (200) diffraction peaks rapidly died, and additional peaks emerged at 21.02°, 23.50°, 25.29°, 29.55°, and 33.9°, belonging to PbBr2 [52,53]. Therefore in the present work, we have not performed photocatalytic degradation of MB using pristine CPB PQDs. But CPB PQDs encapsulated with TiO2 not only provide adequate protection from water instability but efficiently allow a charge migration to the surface of the photocatalyst. Therefore the photocatalytic degradation experiment of CPB@10-TiO2, CPB@30-TiO2 and CPB@50-TiO2 samples successfully carried out under sunlight. Fig. 5 shows the UV–Vis spectra representing the photocatalytic degradation of MB dye using (a) pristine TiO2, (b) CPB@10-TiO2, (c) CPB@30-TiO2, and (d) CPB@50-TiO2 nanocomposite under natural sunlight illumination. The absorbance peak intensity of MB at the wavelength 658 nm was measured after each 60 min of sunlight illumination to quantify the degradation process. This photocatalytic experiment using CPB@TiO2 under natural sunlight was carried out for 300 min. These UV-Visible absorbance spectra were used to quantify% degradation to identify the most efficient photocatalyst among the tested samples. Fig. 6 a shows the comparative% degradation of MB w.r.t. illumination time for pristine TiO2, CPB@10-TiO2, CPB@30-TiO2, and CPB@50-TiO2 samples. Among the samples, the CPB@30-TiO2 shows the maximum degradation of 78%, while the CPB@10-TiO2, CPB@50-TiO2, and pristine TiO2 offers ultimate degradation 68%, 59% and 47%, respectively. The Langmuir-Hinshelwood (L-H) model has been widely used to determine the relationship between dye's degradation rate and concentration. The comparative plot of ln (C0/Ct) versus illumination time for pristine TiO2, CPB@10-TiO2, CPB@30-TiO2, and CPB@50-TiO2 samples under natural sunlight is presented in Fig. 6b. The maximum rate constant of 0.005 /min in natural sunlight is observed for CPB@30-TiO2 nanocomposite. This shows that the photodegradation rate of CPB@30-TiO2 photocatalyst is higher compared to other samples. Hence we performed the photocatalytic experiment in collected sunlight only for CPB@30-TiO2 sample to further improvement in the efficiency of the detoxification process.

Fig. 7 a shows the schematic demonstration of the arrangement used to investigate the photocatalytic process in collected (intense) sunlight. In this set-up, the lab-made solar concentrator was used to concentrate the diffuse sunlight and the MB solution containing photocatalyst was situated away from the focal point of the concentrator. In this way, the photocatalytic experiment was carried out by converting outdoor natural (diffuse) sunlight into collected (intense) sunlight. The previous report suggests that the photodegradation rate could increase linearly at low light intensities. Whereas the degradation rate could depend on the square root of the light intensity at intermediate light intensities [54]. This indicates that the rate of charge carrier generation and rate of recombination depends on the intensity of light. On the other hand, the intensity of the sunlight can also affect the temperature of the solution. As a result, we have simultaneously monitored the intensity and temperature of MB solution containing CPB@30-TiO2 while performing the photocatalysis experiment in collected sunlight. The variation in light intensity and temperature w.r.t illumination time of the polluted water is provided in the natural and collected sunlight and mentioned in the supporting information Table S2 and Table S3, respectively. Table S2 shows that at the maximum intensity of 74 mW/cm2 the corresponding solution temperature was 40 °C. In comparison, the solution temperature reached 63 °C at the maximum intensity of 103 mW/cm2 in the collected sunlight. Hashimoto et al. [55] suggest that the photocatalytic reaction temperature increase generally increases photocatalytic activity below 80 °C. However, when the reaction temperature is higher than 80 °C, the adsorption of organic molecules on the photocatalyst collapse, promoting charge carriers recombination and results in insufficient photocatalytic activity. In the present work, the reaction temperature below 70 °C could accelerate the photocatalytic process. Our data also reveals that the 103 mW/cm2 intensity of the collected sunlight is close to the intensity equivalent to one sun usually used in the laboratory experiment. These results indicate that for the photocatalytic investigation at outdoor ambient conditions, an arrangement of collected sunlight can fulfil the requirement of one sun intensity. To ensure the photocatalytic efficiency of CPB PQD/TiO2 nanocomposite in collected sunlight, the photocatalytic experiment was performed using a solar collector set-up (Fig. 5a). The UV–Vis absorbance spectra taken after each 10 min of collected sunlight illumination are shown in Fig. 7b. As observed, the CPB@30-TiO2 photocatalyst effectively decolourizes the MB solution in just 50 min of collected sunlight illumination. The variation of MB degradation using CPB@30-TiO2 photocatalyst below regular and collected sunlight is compared in Fig. 7c. As observed, the CPB@30-TiO2 nanocomposite takes 300 min of sunlight illumination to degrade 78% of MB, whereas it takes only 50 min of collected sunlight to degrade above 99% of MB. This represents that photocatalyst degrades MB more efficiently in the collected sunlight compared to natural sunlight. To inspect the degradation rate, we plotted the variation of ln (C0/Ct) with time for CPB@30-TiO2 nanocomposite for both natural and collected sunlight, as shown in Fig. 7d. The rate constant for CPB@30-TiO2 photocatalyst is 0.0046 /min and 0.108 /min in natural and collected sunlight, respectively. This indicates that the rate of photocatalytic degradation of MB using CPB@30-TiO2 doubled in collected sunlight. The photocatalytic efficiency of the CPB@30-TiO2 nanocomposite photocatalyst of the present work is compared with the literature [7,45,47,56,57], as represented in Table 1 . It identifies that the CPB@30-TiO2 photocatalyst takes a much lower time to degrade MB in intense sunlight among reported Cs-based perovskite photocatalyst. Also, all the synthesis steps are performed in the ambient air, whereas other perovskite halides are processed in the glove box with a nitrogen environment. Hence, our strategy is more promising to reduce the cost of the photocatalysis process for environmental remediation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: A possible mechanism and stability test for photocatalytic reaction
ce:section.ce:para: As observed from the photocatalysis experiments, CPB@30-TiO2 nanocomposite degrades the MB pollutant efficiently than TiO2 alone. This is expected because the CPB act as a light absorber. Further, the favourable band alignment of CPB and TiO2 attributed to heterojunction deteriorate recombination and accelerate charge migration to the surface of the photocatalyst. This was confirmed by the comparative PL measurements of CPB PQDs and CPB@30TiO2 nanocomposite (Fig. S6 of the supporting information). The PL signal of the CPB@30TiO2 nanocomposite exhibits substantial quenching compared to CPB PQDs. The PL quenching attributed to the emerging different non-radiative channel due to charge transfer from CPB to TiO2 phase. This charge transport between the CPB and TiO2 phase is possible due to suitable band alignment. Similar PL characteristics explaining the photogenerated charge carrier migration and recombination in nanocomposite heterojunction supports present results [58]. The photogenerated charge carriers migrated to the surface of the photocatalyst are responsible for the generation of highly reactive radical species. In this process, O2 molecules adsorbed on the surface of the CPB@TiO2 react with electrons in the conduction band (CB) of TiO2 to generate ̇OH radicals. Subsequently, the holes in the valance band (VB) could oxidize OH¯ or H2O to form ̇OH radicals. These hydroxyl radicals destruct MB organic molecules and reduce the concentration of MB, as supported by earlier reports [46,47].

We have performed transient photocurrent measurements to understand the difference in photocatalytic degradation efficiency of CPB@30-TiO2 in natural and collected sunlight. The experiment was performed by coating CPB@30-TiO2 nanoparticles thin film on FTO substrate using the drop-casting method to make the photoanodes. This photoelectrochemical (PEC) experiment was conducted in two different intensities as 73 mW/cm2 and 103 mW/cm2. These intensities match the criteria of outdoor illumination conditions hence called natural and collected sunlight, respectively. The transient photocurrent response for CPB@30-TiO2 thin film was measured in 0.1 M Na2SO4 electrolyte. The light was illuminated in the ONOFF cycle on the sample, and the current was measured with time, as shown in Fig. 8 a. It was observed that high photocurrent was generated for CPB@30-TiO2 thin film in collected sunlight compared to regular sunlight. Based on these results, we proposed a mechanism of the photodegradation of MB using CPB@30-TiO2 nanocomposite under natural and collected sunlight. It is schematically shown in Fig. 8b and 8c. Application of collected sunlight significantly improves absorption of CPB@30-TiO2 nanocomposite (Fig. 8c) compared to the natural sunlight illumination (Fig. 8c). As a result, more number of electron-hole pairs were generated in CPB@30-TiO2 nanocomposite under collected sunlight. This leads to efficient generate reactive radical species such as OH− and O−2, further aiding the decomposition of contaminants in collected sunlight. The structural stability of CPB@30-TiO2 materials after repeating the photocatalysis experiments under collected sunlight was confirmed with the XRD. The comparative XRD pattern of CPB@30-TiO2 nanocomposite before and after the photodegradation experiment under normal and collected sunlight is shown in Fig. 8d. As observed, the recycled CPB@30-TiO2 nanocomposite still have strong peak intensities and good crystallinity similar to the as-prepared sample. The unchanged XRD pattern of CPB@30-TiO2 nanocomposite shows excellent photocatalytic stability in MB containing water under intense sunlight. This show that TiO2 encapsulation of CPB QDs efficiently protects from surface decomposition and maintain the original optical properties of CPB QDs under photocatalytic cycles. Overall present results confirm that an efficient CPB@30TiO2 nanocomposite photocatalyst satisfies necessary aspects like nanostructured particles, high crystallinity, efficient charge separation, high absorption and stability.

In conclusion, ambient synthesis of CPB QDs @ TiO2 nanocomposite carried out using a simple antisolvent precipitation method. The as-synthesized nanocomposites were successfully utilized for photocatalytic degradation of an MB toxic organic dye under natural and collected sunlight irradiation. Among the samples, CPB@30-TiO2 nanocomposite shows effectively degrade MB dye under natural sunlight. However, the same photocatalyst exhibited impressive degradation of MB in 50 min under collected sunlight. A plausible mechanism has been proposed in this regard to understand the photocatalytic reactions taking place during the photocatalysis process. The remarkable photocatalytic performance of CPB@30-TiO2 nanocomposite could be attributed to the large absorption, enhanced photogenerated charge carriers and improved charge separation. The photocatalyst can be reused multiple times as the stability of the CPB@TiO2 was confirmed by the XRD pattern. In short, our work demonstrated an exceptional photocatalytic activity of CPB@TiO2 nanocomposite under collected sunlight driven degradation of organic dye pollutants.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The author ADS would like to thank the Department of Science and Technology (DST), Ministry of Science and Technology of India, for the research grant and DST-INSPIRE Faculty Award No. DST/INSPIRE/04/2015/002601. The author SKK thanks INSA, N. Delhi, India, for INSA Sr. Scientist position. The authors are thankful to Dr. Dattatray Late, NCL Pune for providing TEM facility.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.materresbull.2021.111433.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhao Y., Wang Y., Liang X., Shi H., Wang C., Fan J., Hu X., Liu E., Enhanced photocatalytic activity of Ag-CsPbBr3/CN composite for broad spectrum photocatalytic degradation of cephalosporin antibiotics 7-ACA, Appl. Catal. B- Environ.2019, 247, 57–69.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhang, Z., Liang, Y., Huang, H., Liu, X., Li, Q., Chen, L., Xu, D., Stable and highly efficient photocatalysis with lead-free double-perovskite of Cs2AgBiBr6, Angew. Chem. Int. Ed.2019, 58, 7263–7267.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Flash photo stimulation of human neural stem cells on graphene/TiO2 heterojunction for differentiation into neurons

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Akhavan O., Ghaderia E., Flash photo stimulation of human neural stem cells on graphene/TiO2 heterojunction for differentiation into neurons, Nanoscale, 2013, 5, 10316–10326.
